coredata.dc:title: Advanced adsorbent-adsorbate pairs for sustainable and energy-efficient adsorption refrigeration in net-zero buildings: Working-Pair performance mapping, AI-driven materials screening, and system integration

coredata.dc:description: The growing demand for sustainable, energy-efficient cooling, driven by global warming and the transition to net-zero buildings, has renewed interest in adsorption refrigeration systems. These thermally driven technologies can exploit low-grade waste heat and solar thermal energy while using low-GWP working fluids, offering a compelling alternative to conventional vapor- compression cooling. However, large footprint, high component cost, and modest performance still hinder widespread deployment, largely due to limited heat and mass transfer in adsorption beds and slow sorption-desorption kinetics. Recent progress spans (i) advanced adsorbent- adsorbate working pairs (e.g., porous frameworks, salt-hybrid/composite adsorbents, and tailored sorbents), (ii) bed-scale intensification strategies (high-conductivity composites, coatings, structured adsorbents, finned/metal-foam exchangers, and additive-manufactured architectures), and (iii) improved cycle designs (heat/mass recovery, multi-bed and multi-stage configurations) that collectively raise COP and SCP. To make advanced working pairs and AI-driven material innovations central, and comparable across studies, this review compiles a unified working-pair database and introduces performance maps linking equilibrium/kinetic/thermophysical properties to operating windows (regeneration temperature, pressure lift, and achievable cooling capacity). We further present a concrete AI screening and down-selection workflow, covering data curation, descriptor selection, surrogate modeling, uncertainty-aware multi-objective optimization (COP-SCP-cost-temperature constraints), and experimental/TEA-informed validation. Finally, standardized, normalized comparison tables are provided to reconcile boundary-condition differences and directly connect material selection to cycle choice and bed design. By integrating materials discovery, AI-enabled design, and system-level engineering, this review offers an actionable framework to accelerate scalable adsorption cooling for sustainable, net-zero built environments.

row.entry: Why it matters at system level
Δw under fixed building window (e.g., Tevap 12 °C; Tcond/Tads 32 °C; Tdes 60–90 °C) + isotherm source
Governs specific cooling potential; sets lower bound on bed mass/volume
Tdes,min to achieve a target Δw (or target pressure lift) within the window
Determines compatibility with solar thermal / waste heat; affects net-zero feasibility
t₉₀ or characteristic time constant at adsorption/desorption conditions; mass transfer coefficient if available
Controls cycle time and achievable SCP; impacts valve/bed switching strategy
keff (powder vs consolidated/composite); contact resistance notes
Limits power density; affects fin/foam/heat exchanger design complexity
Bulk density, pellet density, shaped body porosity
Determines volumetric capacity and footprint (critical for buildings)
Stability under cycling; water tolerance; structural retention; salt migration/hysteresis
Predicts lifetime, maintenance burden, and performance drift
Material compatibility notes (metals, seals); corrosion inhibitors if needed
Affects component cost and reliability; critical for ammonia/salts
Toxicity/flammability class, operating pressure range; GWP/ODP (if relevant)
Drives code compliance and building adoption
Synthesis route, shaping/coating feasibility; raw material availability
Determines scalability and CAPEX; shapes the “real” technology potential

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The growing demand for sustainable, energy-efficient cooling, driven by global warming and the transition to net-zero buildings, has renewed interest in adsorption refrigeration systems. These thermally driven technologies can exploit low-grade waste heat and solar thermal energy while using low-GWP working fluids, offering a compelling alternative to conventional vapor- compression cooling. However, large footprint, high component cost, and modest performance still hinder widespread deployment, largely due to limited heat and mass transfer in adsorption beds and slow sorption-desorption kinetics. Recent progress spans (i) advanced adsorbent- adsorbate working pairs (e.g., porous frameworks, salt-hybrid/composite adsorbents, and tailored sorbents), (ii) bed-scale intensification strategies (high-conductivity composites, coatings, structured adsorbents, finned/metal-foam exchangers, and additive-manufactured architectures), and (iii) improved cycle designs (heat/mass recovery, multi-bed and multi-stage configurations) that collectively raise COP and SCP. To make advanced working pairs and AI-driven material innovations central, and comparable across studies, this review compiles a unified working-pair database and introduces performance maps linking equilibrium/kinetic/thermophysical properties to operating windows (regeneration temperature, pressure lift, and achievable cooling capacity). We further present a concrete AI screening and down-selection workflow, covering data curation, descriptor selection, surrogate modeling, uncertainty-aware multi-objective optimization (COP-SCP-cost-temperature constraints), and experimental/TEA-informed validation. Finally, standardized, normalized comparison tables are provided to reconcile boundary-condition differences and directly connect material selection to cycle choice and bed design. By integrating materials discovery, AI-enabled design, and system-level engineering, this review offers an actionable framework to accelerate scalable adsorption cooling for sustainable, net-zero built environments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to rapid urbanization and industrialization, there is an increasing need for cooling applications day by day. Cooling is also needed for the development of food and lifesaving medicine storage facilities. According to estimates from the International Institute of Refrigeration (IIR), cooling and air conditioning applications use 15 % of all power produced globally [1]. At present, most of the cooling requirements are provided by conventional vapor compression cooling systems that are powered by dwindling fossil fuel sources. These systems utilize hazardous chlorofluorocarbon (CFC) and hydrofluorocarbon (HFC) refrigerants. Concerns over emissions of greenhouse gases, global warming, and climate change are now very real. The problem is due to the increasing use of fossil fuels and the emission of CFC and HFC gases [2,3]. Furthermore, industry wastes almost 67 % of its generated energy [4]. Numerous renewable energy resources remain underutilized. As a result, research on the efficient use of waste heat sources and low-grade solar energy has grown significantly [5]. Because it can effectively utilize waste heat from cars, factories, and other renewable heat sources and employs ecologically acceptable refrigerants, adsorption technology has strong potential to solve the mounting problems of fuel scarcity, environmental issues, and climate change [6,7]. This feature enables adsorption cooling to support sustainable cooling for medications and preserve food in remote locations without access to electricity, giving it significant potential to save energy. Nevertheless, adsorption cooling systems are used only to a limited extent and are not yet widely commercialized due to their enormous size, increased expense, and still-poor specific cooling capacity and coefficients of performance.

The sluggish mass and heat transfer rate of the adsorbent bed, along with its low adsorption capacity, primarily contribute to these limitations. While long cycle durations are sometimes considered a drawback, in certain cases they can actually be advantageous, as they allow for better utilization of heat input and reduction of switching losses. To tackle the existing challenges, researchers are concentrating on various domains, including the creation of efficient adsorbent materials and the identification of suitable adsorption pairs, the formulation of effective adsorption cycles, the improvement of heat exchanger configurations, and the optimization of cooling system operational and design parameters [8]. Consequently, a multitude of advanced adsorption cycles have been developed to enhance cooling capacities and facilitate ongoing cooling [9–15]. Improved heat transfer in the adsorber bed raises the bed's overall heat transfer performance. Similar to this, improved mass transfer in the adsorber bed shortens the time it takes for refrigerant to adsorb and desorb from the adsorbent. Better heat and mass transfer in the adsorbent beds consequently increase the specific cooling and energy conversion efficiency of the system [16]. Such improvement is only possible with an effective adsorber bed design.

This work offers a state-of-the-art evaluation of complex adsorption cycles and developments in adsorbent bed design to boost the efficacy of adsorption cooling applications. Various advanced adsorption cycles for adsorption cooling systems and techniques to enhance heat and mass transfer rates in adsorption beds have been highlighted and discussed. Research on utilizing artificial intelligence (AI) methods for adsorption cooling system design and optimization has also been reported. These methods could potentially compensate for the drawbacks of experimental and computational methods. The breadth of knowledge reported in this work provides a launchpad for further advancements in adsorption system development.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Global publication trends in adsorption cooling systems
ce:section.ce:para: A bibliometric analysis was conducted using the Scopus database to map research trends in adsorption-based cooling technologies. The search query was applied to the TITLE-ABS-KEY fields and included keywords such as “adsorption cooling,” “adsorbent–adsorbate pairs,” and “sustainable refrigeration.” To ensure the relevance and quality of the dataset, filters were applied to include only research and review articles (LIMIT-TO(DOCTYPE, “ar”) OR LIMIT-TO(DOCTYPE, “re”)) published in the English language (LIMIT-TO(LANGUAGE, “English”)) over the period 2005–2025. The resulting publication trend indicates a steady growth in research activity. Annual publications remained below 20 papers per year until 2010, followed by a noticeable increase after 2015, reflecting growing interest in sustainable cooling technologies. The number of publications reached a peak of 73 in 2025 (Fig. 1 a).

The keyword co-occurrence map highlights major themes around adsorption, activated carbon, solar energy, and coefficient of performance, indicating emphasis on cycle enhancement, effective adsorbent-adsorbate pairs, and solar integration; recent terms such as machine learning, heat exchanger design, and desalination systems point to multidisciplinary advances (Fig. 1b). Country collaboration analysis shows China, India, and Japan as leading contributors, with strong links across Asia and the Middle East and active participation from Egypt, Saudi Arabia, the United Kingdom, and the United States (Fig. 1c). Journal mapping indicates that most studies appear in Applied Thermal Engineering, Applied Energy, Renewable and Sustainable Energy Reviews, Renewable Energy, and Energies, underscoring the field’s thermal-science and sustainability focus (Fig. 1d). Overall, the evidence suggests that adsorption cooling has moved from a niche topic to a mature, fast-growing area. It is supported by international collaboration and the increasing use of data-driven methods for design and optimization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working-pair selection for net-zero buildings: from material properties to system operating windows
ce:section.ce:para: The ultimate performance of adsorption refrigeration is dictated by thermodynamics and kinetics of the chosen adsorbent adsorbate (working) pair in the presence of building-relevant boundary conditions. Although advanced cycles and intensified beds should be able to substantially enhance the heat/mass transfer and minimize the dead-time, these methods cannot remedy a working pair that (i) needs regeneration temperatures impractical in the presence of common solar thermal, low-grade waste heat, (ii) has inadequate working capacity within desired evaporator/condensation temperatures, or (iii) has sluggish uptake/release reaction or hydrothermal degradation, or poor manufacturability. Thus, cycle architecture and bed design should not be done before working-pair selection is in place so that later innovations in the system do not counteract, but enhance the inherent material envelope.

To formalize the working-pair selection of net-zero buildings, this subsection gives: (i) standard selection criteria, (ii) normalized definition of the operating-window as representative of building cooling, (iii) performance map of materials as a function of regeneration temperature and usable uptake, and (iv) a critical comparison table which requires apples-to- apples assessment across different studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Building-relevant operating windows and normalization protocol
ce:section.ce:para:
For fair comparison across the literature, working pairs should be evaluated under a fixed “building window” that reflects typical comfort-cooling constraints and realistic heat-source temperatures. A practical normalization window is:
i. Evaporator temperature (Tevap): 10–15 °C (comfort cooling / chilled water supply range)
ii. Condenser temperature (Tcond): 30–35 °C (air-cooled or cooling-tower-assisted rejection)
iii. Adsorption temperature (Tads): Tcond (bed adsorption often near heat-rejection temperature)
iv. Desorption temperature (Tdes): swept over 60–90 °C (solar thermal + low-grade waste heat)

Within this window, define a normalized usable working capacity (per kg adsorbent) as:

Here, w (P, T) is equilibrium uptake (desorption and adsorption stage) at pressure P and temperature T, and Psat(T) is the refrigerant saturation pressure at temperature T. This definition directly connects material equilibrium behavior to the adsorption-cycle pressure lift and allows consistent reporting even when system test rigs and cycle times vary across publications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection criteria for working pairs in net-zero buildings
ce:section.ce:para: Working-pair selection must account for thermodynamics, kinetics, transport, stability, safety, and scalability, not only peak COP/SCP reported under favorable lab conditions. Table 1 summarizes criteria and how each should be quantified or at least consistently described.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material performance map for building windows
ce:section.ce:para: To move beyond qualitative lists, working pairs should be visualized on a performance map that captures the central design trade-off in net-zero buildings: low regeneration temperature vs. high usable working capacity (with kinetics/stability as secondary screens).

Fig. 2 . shows the working-pair performance map for net-zero building operating windows. The x-axis reports the minimum regeneration temperature, Tdes,min, required to reach a threshold usable working capacity (Δw ≥ Δw*) under fixed conditions (Tevap = 12 °C, Tcond = Tads = 32 °C). The y-axis reports the usable working capacity Δw at a reference regeneration temperature (Tdes = 80 °C) for the same window, computed from equilibrium uptake as Δw = w(Pevap,sat(Tevap), Tads) - w(Pcond,sat(Tcond), Tdes). Marker shape denotes adsorbent family and color denotes refrigerant. This map provides a direct, system-relevant basis for selecting working pairs compatible with low-grade heat while highlighting trade-offs between regeneration temperature and cooling potential.

Having identified candidate working pairs compatible with building operating windows and low-grade heat, the next design step is selecting a cycle architecture (heat/mass recovery, multi- bed, multi-stage) that best leverages the pair’s thermodynamic envelope and kinetics, followed by bed-scale intensification to realize that envelope at high power density.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Different systems for adsorption cooling technology
ce:section.ce:para: One bed is used to run the basic adsorption cooling cycle, which is highly intermittent. This cycle has low performances, and the cooling output is not continuous. Fig. 3 illustrates the simple cycle and a cooling output pattern, which highlights its intermittent nature. The single- bed basic cycle produces intermittent cooling, whereas a two-bed configuration provides nearly continuous cooling by staggering the adsorption-desorption phases. Continuous cooling can be achieved with two or more beds. Heat recovery is not an option in the basic cycle. Multi-beds could be used to achieve a significantly better COP and substantially more heat recovery. To make the adsorption system economically feasible, adsorption cycles require sufficiently higher COP and SCP. Consequently, a great deal of research has been done on the basic cycle of adsorption refrigeration. Fig. 4 depicts the categorization of adsorption refrigeration systems. The advanced adsorption cycles have been described in full detail. There are two main types of adsorption systems, namely, physisorption and chemisorption. The existing physisorption system is divided into open and closed; the closed catalytic cycle can be divided into single- and multi-bed. More complex cycles use different approaches like mass recovery, heat recovery, combined mass and heat recovery, thermal wave, convective thermal wave, cascading, and multi-stage to improve on the performance. Alternatively, systems in adsorption cooling tubes have been made.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Use of heat recovery cycle in adsorption cooling
ce:section.ce:para: In the heat recovery cycle, using two or more adsorbent beds in the adsorption process allows for quasi-continuous cooling. The adsorbent bed to be cooled will transfer sensible heat as well as adsorption heat to the adsorber to be heated. This is analogous to the solution heat exchanger used in vapor absorption refrigeration. By raising the cooling output and lowering the heat input, this reduces parasitic losses such as the excess heat rejected to the surroundings, the energy wasted in reheating the adsorbent bed and the additional work required for switching and pumping of heat transfer fluid and thereby raises the system's coefficient of performance (COP). In a cycle with heat recovery or a uniform temperature process, a heat transfer fluid circulates through the hot and cold adsorbers in series so that heat is transferred from the hot reactor to the cold reactor.

Adopting this cycle can recover approximately 30 % of the total energy transferred to each reactor, according to research. Fig. 5 (a-b) depicts a quasi-continuous adsorption refrigeration system. The heat source is connected to the adsorbent (A) in order to heat and desorb the refrigerant vapor from the adsorbent (desorption phase). The refrigerant vapor is sent to the evaporator after cooling in the condenser. There, it evaporates and absorbs heat from the region that needs to be cooled to provide the cooling effect. In the same amount of time, the refrigerant vapor that emerges from the evaporator is absorbed by adsorber (B). The heat from condensation and adsorption is eliminated by cooling water. As a result, adsorber B will always be in the adsorption process (cooling process), whereas adsorber A is in the desorption process (heating process). The isosteric heating and cooling of adsorbers occurs between these intervals. To enhance cycle performance, adsorbers A and B are connected to recover heat at the conclusion of each desorption phase. Maggio, Freni, and Restuccia [17] developed a two- dimensional mathematical model of an adsorption cooler consisting of a double-consolidated adsorbent bed with internal heat recovery.

The effectiveness of a single-bed cooling system and a two-bed adsorption cooling system was examined in this study. The simulated findings demonstrated that while the cycle time increased and the cooling COP increased in comparison to the single-bed design, the specific cooling capacity decreased. In their base case, the double-bed cycle with internal heat recovery increased the cooling COP from 0.445 (single-bed) to 0.635 (double-bed), an absolute gain of 0.190, about 43 % relative improvement, while the specific cooling power decreased from 1220.32 to 838.77 W kg⁻¹ (31 % drop) and the cycle time rose from 548.01 to 797.23 s (46 % increase). It was determined that the specific cooling capacity had less of an impact on the COP than the change in the heat recovery phase's extent. Additionally, they conducted a sensitivity analysis to look into the effects of the important mass transfer and heat transfer parameters to optimize the performance of the recommended double-bed system with internal heat recovery.

A distributed-parameter model of a two-bed silica gel-water adsorption chiller as shown in Fig. 6 was created by Wang and Chua [18], who also looked into the effectiveness of passive and water-circulation heat-recovery schemes on the chiller's performance under rated operating circumstances. Both the mentioned passive heat recovery scheme and the Nishiyodo water- circulation heat recovery scheme are equally effective. Additionally, experimental data were used to validate the simulated results. The passive heat recovery scheme can be easily included in commercial chillers with minor modifications to the system management logic. It may even be able to replace the Nishiyodo method for recovering heat through water circulation at the design stage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption cooling with mass recovery cycle
ce:section.ce:para: At the conclusion of each half-cycle, the vapor can also be recovered internally in addition to through heat recovery. The vapour pressure inside the adsorber linked to the condenser is significantly higher than the vapour pressure inside the adsorber connected to the evaporator at the end of each half-cycle in a quasi-continuous system made up of two adsorbers running out of phase. At the beginning of the second half-cycle, the high pressure adsorber needs to be depressurized and the low pressure needs to be pressurized. A part of this depressurization- pressurization process can be made to occur internally by connecting the two adsorbers to each other, so that vapour from high pressure adsorber flows into the low-pressure adsorber, till the pressures become equal. This process is very rapid as it involves only mass transfer. Research indicates that the mass recovery process or internal vapor recovery can be crucial in enhancing the efficiency of adsorption cooling [19]. Fig. 7 shows the mass recovery adsorption cooling system.

Akahira et al. [20] looked at the effectiveness of the two-bed silica gel-water adsorption cooling cycle with mass recovery technique. The scientists found that the chilled water outlet temperature of the mass recovery cycle was lower than that of the single stage cycle. It was shown that the mass recovery cycle performed better at low regeneration temperatures and had a larger cooling capacity than the conventional single-stage cycle. The mass recovery cycle with heating and cooling has a lower coefficient of performance (COP) than other cycles. However, COP loses significance if the driving source is a waste heat source. Unless the average chilled water outlet temperature rises, it is appropriate to take a somewhat longer period for the mass recovery cycle with heating and cooling in order to improve the SCP value. The study further reported that COP decreases as the mass recovery process time increases: for example, when the average chilled water outlet was fixed at 7 °C, the COP of the conventional mass recovery cycle was about 0.46, but only 0.36 in the mass recovery cycle with heating and cooling (22 % lower), even though the cooling capacity increased from 5.8 to 6.3 kW under the same conditions [20]. Similar researchers created an experimental prototype machine and data collected during experiments to verify the simulation results [21]. Pons and Poyelle [22] studied an enhanced adsorption cycle in which vapor is internally recovered between the two adsorbers to boost the cooling power. The mass circulation is enhanced by the mass recovery process, which makes use of the pressure differential between the adsorber and desorber beds.

Fig. 8 illustrates the adsorption refrigeration cycle, showing alternating heating/desorption and cooling/adsorption processes. The schematic representation highlights the main components adsorbers, condenser, and evaporator and their heat interactions with external sources. The heat source temperature in this investigation was 240 °C, which was greater than the temperature of low-grade waste heat. Zeolite and water were utilized as the adsorbent pair. This adsorbent-adsorbate combination was therefore unsuitable for waste heat sources with low temperatures. Wang [23] examined the vapor recovery cycle's performance using a pair of activated carbon and methanol and came to the conclusion that the mass recovery cycle worked well at low regenerating temperatures.

Fig. 9 demonstrates how refrigerant transfer between adsorbers enhances performance. By enabling mass and heat recovery, the cycle improves cooling capacity and coefficient of performance compared to the basic cycle. Furthermore, it was shown that mass recovery might increase the coefficient of performance (COP) by 10 % when used in heat recovery adsorption cooling operations. In addition, the author suggested that mass recovery work well in situations where there is high condensing temperature, low evaporation temperature, and low generation temperature. Khan et al. [24] used a modeling tool to study a three-bed adsorption cooling system with a mass recovery process powered by solar or waste heat as shown in Fig. 10 .

The chiller was operated using heat from solar panels or waste heat in the range of 60–90 °C, with a cooling source maintained at 30 °C for air conditioning. Performance comparisons were conducted between the three-bed adsorption chiller with the mass recovery technique and the three-bed chiller without mass recovery. The cooling effect and solar/waste heat recovery efficiency of the adsorption chiller with mass recovery were found to be superior across the studied heat source temperature range. In particular, the recommended chiller achieved a significantly higher COP at lower driving temperatures: at 60 °C, the COP improved from about 0.15 (without mass recovery) to 0.21 (with mass recovery), and at 70 °C it increased from 0.18 to 0.25 representing a gain of roughly 35–40 %.

The effectiveness of a complex three-bed adsorption cooling system with a mass recovery cycle and pairing of silica gel and water was investigated experimentally [25]. The temperature and pressure of the chiller's different parts were examined in order to see how it behaved dynamically. Comparing the recommended cooler's COP and SCP values to those of a traditional single-stage cooler, it was found that the suggested cooler's values were superior for low-temperature heat sources below 75 °C. Pan et al. [26] developed a silica gel-water adsorption chiller and carried out an experimental study to identify sources of waste heat at very low temperatures using a mass recovery technique. They discovered that the introduction of mass recovery processes-one in the adsorber bed and another in the desorber bed-increases the cyclic adsorption quantity of water. According to experimental research, it is operationally possible and performs well with waste heat sources that are heated to extremely low temperatures-between 56.2 and 74.9 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heat and mass recovery refrigeration cycle
ce:section.ce:para: For adsorption refrigeration systems to be both practically and economically feasible, adsorption cycles must have high enough values of COP and SCP. As a result, various advancements on the fundamental adsorption refrigeration cycle have been studied. It is possible to get a twofold improvement in cycle performance by combining the regenerative heat recovery and internal vapor mass recovery processes in a single cycle. A schematic representation of the mass recovery/heat and mass recovery cycle is shown in Fig. 11 . The mass recovery process is followed by the heat recovery process. The technique of combining both mass and heat recovery processes genuinely results in a significantly stronger refrigeration effect and a better COP of the double effect mass and heat recovering system when compared to either mass only or heat only recovery systems. Mass and heat recovery cycle times will be significantly reduced, which will improve the cycle and result in higher cooling and heating power [19]. Lu et al. [27] carried out experimental research on a 2-bed adsorption system with mass and heat- pipe heat recovery process. The suggested cycle was driven by solar energy or engine waste heat and employed a compound adsorbent (a combination of CaCl2 and activated carbon) and ammonia pair. The heat pipe recovery method proved the value of the heat pipe's high thermal conductivity and the lack of an additional working liquid needed for heat transfer when compared to other heat recovery schemes. The heat recovery provided the extra heat needed for the mass recovery. The results showed that the SCP of the cycle with the combination of mass and heat recovery outperformed the traditional cycle with either mass recovery or heat recovery alone. It was also determined that the cycle time was shortened in addition to improving refrigeration performance when mass and heat recovery were coupled. Compared to 60, 120, and 150 s of recovery time, the system performed better at 90 s. Lu et al.'s study [28] examined the effects of heat recovery and mass recovery on two adsorption cooling systems using working pairs of microporous silica gel and water and macro-porous silica gel and LiCl-methanol.

Through experimentation, the adsorption cooling was examined and contrasted. The outcomes of the experiment demonstrated that while the heat recovery process more successfully raises the system's coefficient of performance, the mass recovery method greatly increases cooling capacity. When the temperatures of the hot water inlet, cold water inlet, chilled water outlet, and mass recovery time are approximately 80 °C, 30 °C, 15 °C, and 60 s, respectively, the COP and SCP of the system without mass recovery are 1.2 kW and 0.2, while they are 1.6 kW and 0.13 of the system with mass recovery scheme. Mass recovery reduces the heating capacity by 2.7 % while increasing the cooling capacity by 36.4 %. While heat recovery increases cooling capacity by 6.3 %, it reduces heating capacity by 19.8 % in systems with heat recovery schemes compared to those without. The study also demonstrated that the silica gel/LiCl-methanol adsorption cooling cycle had a higher SCP and could be applied to cold storage due to its greater working pressure and adsorption capacity.

The dashed line represents thermal fluid movement; the solid line and valve between the beds process mass recovery; the dashed line between the beds processes heat recovery. An analytical model for a two-bed silica gel-water-based adsorption cooling cycle was created by Wang et al. [29]. Three vacuum chambers were used in this adsorption chiller: one heat pipe functioning as the evaporator and two adsorptions/desorption (or evaporation/condensation) vacuum chambers. The authors looked into how operating temperatures, heat, and mass recovery affected cooling efficiency. The highest COP was observed when the maximum bed temperature was between 60 and 80 °C. At a chilled water intake temperature of 20 °C, a cooling capacity of 12.6 kW and a COP of 0.65 were discovered. Furthermore, it was found that the waste heat source, which operates at temperatures lower than 60 °C, was more affected by the mass recovery technique.

To study the effect of internal and external mass transfer on the cooling performance of the adsorption cycle, Leong and Liu [30] developed a numerical transient model that described an adsorption refrigeration system with zeolite-water pair and included a combined heat and mass recovery cycle. The model provides a detailed description of the two-dimensional heat and mass transfer balance equations in the adsorber, which are solved using the control volume approach. The linear driving force equation in this model describes the micro mass transfer constraint.

The numerical findings demonstrated that, under the given operating conditions, the mass recovery period lasted only roughly 50 s, as opposed to the entire cycle time. The COP and SCP can be enhanced by roughly 6 % and 7 %, respectively, with mass recovery alone, in comparison to the basic cycle. The computed values of the COP and SCP for the combined heat and mass recovery cycle under the specified conditions are 0.651 and 27.58 W/kg, respectively. According to the simulated results, there is a notable decrease in SCP of around 40 % along with a notable rise in COP of roughly 47 % when compared to the basic cycle. Ng et al. [31] have examined a four-bed adsorption cooling system utilizing passive heat and mass recovery methods in a related study. It was found that the system COP might increase to as much as 48 % when the heat and mass recovery methods were implemented at the highest cooling capacity rating point.

The review of the literature led to the conclusion that a variety of methods for recovering heat and mass have been put forth, and that integrating these processes into adsorption cooling systems is a highly wise move for improving system efficiency. In order to study the operating parameters and performances of the heat and mass recovery processes in terms of the inlet and outlet temperatures as well as the transferred heat of the two adsorber beds and two evaporators, Pan and Wang [32] selected a mass recovery-like procedure between two evaporators and a serial heat recovery process between two adsorber beds. The experimental findings demonstrated that the heat and mass recovery processes are greatly impacted by the remaining heat transfer fluid in the evaporators and adsorbers, respectively. Additionally, it was shown that the ideal ranges for mass recovery time and heat are 5–50 s and 25–45 s, respectively. The following conclusions can be made about heat and mass recovery cycles based on theoretical and experimental research:
•
The performances of these cycles were superior to those of conventional cycles with heat or mass recovery techniques independently.
•
The cycle time is also reduced.

Limiting temperatures of the chilled water outlet of a conventional two-bed adsorption chiller differ considerably during steady-state cyclic running. Although such variability can be tolerated with regard to general comfort cooling or rough process cooling, it becomes intolerable in application such as dehumidification, precision air conditioning or pharmaceutical storage, where consistency in chilled water temperature is required to assure reliability in the processes and product quality. Furthermore, the capability of a two-bed adsorption chiller to obtain waste heat and feed useful cooling is relatively low and this further inhibits its effectiveness. In order to take advantage of these shortcomings and maximize chilling capacity, multi-bed regenerative adsorption chillers are being used, and they could simultaneously reduce variations in outlet temperature and increase the efficiency of the waste heat recovery.

Some researchers have reported both theoretical and experimental investigations of multi- bed adsorption cooling systems. Chua et al.'s design of a multi-bed regenerative adsorption chiller [33] decreased temperature fluctuations in the chilled water outflow and increased the effectiveness of waste heat recovery. In this study, the performances of four-bed and six-bed cooling systems were compared to that of a two-bed cooling system. It was found that a four- bed chiller produced about 70 % more cooling capacity than a standard two-bed chiller for the same waste heat source, flow rate, and inlet temperature, while a six-bed chiller produced 40 % more cooling than a four-bed chiller. However, the adoption of multi-bed systems comes with a substantial cost penalty Chua et al. reported that the equipment cost of a four-bed system was about 1.5 times higher than that of a two-bed system, and a six-bed system was nearly double the cost, owing mainly to the additional number of adsorbent beds, valves, and heat exchangers required.

The performance evaluation of a novel waste heat driven dual evaporator type three-bed adsorption cycle for cooling applications was studied by Miyazaki et al. [34]. This three-bed adsorption system is shown in Fig. 12 . This study examined the effects of cycle time, chilled water inlet and outlet temperatures, and hot water input temperature on the specific cooling capacity (SCC) and COP. The SCC and COP of this three-bed adsorption chiller with a dual evaporator type rose by 50 % and 70 %, respectively, under the same operating conditions as the two-bed single-stage adsorption chiller. Additionally, it was determined that the dual evaporator type three-bed adsorption chiller performed well at large temperature differences between the chilled water inlet and outlet for example, when the inlet temperature was around 14 °C and the outlet dropped to about 7 °C and that the longer pre-heating/cooling time in this case can minimize the temperature fluctuation of the delivered chilled water.

Uyun et al. [36] examined a novel three-bed mass recovery cycle, both with and without integrated heat recovery, in order to enhance the mass recovery cycle's performance. Unlike earlier studies that also considered three-bed configurations, the novelty here lies in the combination of mass recovery with heat recovery and in systematically analyzing the effect of different desorption mechanisms within the same system. Three silica gel adsorbent beds with various heat-utilization purposes made up the cycle. There are two cycles of adsorbent beds, each with a unique desorption mechanism. The outcomes also demonstrated the existence of an ideal desorption period and adsorber mass distribution point that led to optimal performance. In the range of heat source temperatures from 60 to 90 °C, the suggested cycle performed better than alternative cycles. The performances of AC systems with various working cycles have been summarized in Table 2 .

Table 2 shows that COP and SCP values reported in the literature are strongly affected by operating temperatures and cycle time, so direct comparisons across studies can be misleading. To improve comparability, Table 1 reports a Carnot fraction (ηC = COP/COPCarnot) for studies that provide (or constrain) the driving temperature, using a normalized building window (Te = 12 °C and Tc = 32 °C) as a common basis. Within the low-grade heat range (Th = 40–65 °C), the available data indicate ηC values on the order of 0.19–0.31, and recovery-based cycles (mass recovery, heat recovery, or combined recovery) generally retain a higher fraction of the theoretical limit under low driving temperatures by reducing internal irreversibility and improving effective pressure equalization. In contrast, cycles that deliver higher absolute COP (e.g., cascading or thermal-wave architectures) often rely on higher driving temperatures and/or increased component count. Fig. 13 (a) summarizes the COP-SCP trade-off and highlights that gains in power density frequently coincide with increased cycle complexity (more beds and/or recovery stages).

Heat sources that are either hot or medium in temperature are required for the majority of the adsorption cooling systems discussed above to operate. Alam et al. [43] state that a conventional silica gel-water adsorption cycle cannot function with the driving heat source temperature of 50 °C if the heat sink is 30 °C or higher. Consequently, a cycle that shows promise for utilizing heat sources at low temperatures (45–60 °C) is the multistage adsorption cooling cycle. On the other hand, multi-stage systems perform worse than those using simple cycles. The energy conversion efficiency of multi-stage adsorption refrigeration cycles has been investigated by several researchers. Introduced by Saha et al. [41], the basic two-stage cycle is the simplest form of multi-stage adsorption system. Four adsorber beds, a condenser, and an evaporator make up the two-stage cycle, as illustrated in Fig. 14 . The researchers examined experimental temperature profiles of the heat transfer fluid inputs and outputs. The two-stage cycle was shown to operate efficiently at 55 °C solar/waste heat in conjunction with a 30 °C coolant temperature. Under these conditions, the rated cooling capacity was 3.54 kW, and the COP reached 0.36. Importantly, the study highlighted the feasibility of operating such cycles with low-temperature heat sources, such as those provided by flat plate solar collectors, although in principle the system can be integrated with any suitable heat source. In a different study, Alam et al. [44] developed a simulation model of a two-stage adsorption chiller based on silica gel-water and examined how design and operating parameters influenced system performance. The results indicated that the two-stage adsorption chiller with 12.5 kg of silica gel achieved the maximum COP, and under base conditions, the cooling capacity and specific cooling capacity were proportional and inversely proportional to the silica gel weight, respectively. Although improving the overall heat transfer coefficient can enhance cooling capacity, such enhancement does not always lead to higher COP; in the reported studies, the base run condition identified an optimal coefficient of about 1.5 kW/m²·K. In order to provide air conditioning, Khan et al. [45] investigated a two-stage adsorption cooler with reheat that was driven by low-temperature waste heat sources between 50 and 70 °C and a cooling source at 30 °C.

The performance of the cooler was examined by the authors in relation to the adsorbent mass and the total thermal conductance of the sorption components and evaporator. The investigation revealed that because of the reasonable heating and cooling requirements resulting from batched cycle operation, the overall thermal conductance of sorption elements had a significant impact on the cycle performance. It was determined that when the mass of silica gel decreased, the COP rose. Additionally, the study found that silica gel mass beyond 60 kg was no longer advantageous. In addition to the study mentioned above [45], Khan et al. [46] investigated the effects of the silica gel mass, the overall thermal conductance ratio of the evaporator and sorption element, and the thermal capacitance ratio of the adsorbent bed and inert material on the performance of the two-stage chiller used for re-heating. The simulated findings demonstrated that the overall thermal conductance values of the sorption elements had a significant impact on cycle performance. When the mass of silica gel was comparatively higher, the influence of the thermal capacitance ratio became significant, as an increased adsorbent-to-inert material capacitance ratio led to improved COP but reduced specific cooling power (SCP) due to longer cycle times. Saha et al. [47] studied the performance of low-grade heat-operated advanced three-stage adsorption chiller with regeneration temperature of 50 °C and cooling water temperature of 30 °C.

The system was intended for use in air conditioning, and the chiller comprised six adsorption beds that make use of the silica gel-water working pair. This study examined the effects of operating factors on cooling output, COP, and system efficiency, including temperatures, flow rates, and adsorption-desorption cycle periods. This chiller's primary benefit over other heat- driven chillers was that it could operate with lower regeneration temperature lifts. The three- stage chiller can be run with low-temperature heat sources of 50 and 40 °C combined with cooling sources of 39 and 30 °C, according to the simulation results. The simulated findings also demonstrated that, for the chiller to function properly, heat sources of 50 °C and cooling sources in the range of 35 to 20 °C are required, whereas heat sources of 40 °C require cooling sources in the range of 28 to 20 °C.

In a different study, Saha et al. presented a more sophisticated three-stage adsorption technique [42,48]. These investigations simulated and examined a dual-mode silica gel-water adsorption chiller that efficiently used low temperature solar or waste heat sources of 40–95 °C. The developed chiller could be operated in two ways. Operating as a high-efficiency conventional chiller with a driving heat source temperature of between 60 and 95 °C was the first mode of operation. In the second mode of operation, when the available driving source temperature was between 40 and 60 °C, an advanced three-stage adsorption chiller functioned. The primary inventive characteristic of the dual-mode chiller was its capacity to operate at both low and high regeneration temperature lifts (10 and 50 K), making it a desirable energy-saving option. According to Saha et al., the optimum COP occurs at 50–55 °C (three-stage) and 80–85 °C (single-stage, multi-bed); at these conditions and with coolant at 30 °C, representative COP values are 0.17–0.25 (three-stage at 50 °C) and 0.38–0.56 (single-stage at 80 °C), depending on chilled-water inlet temperature. Khan et al. [35] investigated the performance of a multi-stage, six-bed adsorption chiller with re-heat system that is based on silica gel and water in order to take advantage of low temperature heat sources.

Fig. 15 illustrates the innovative dual-mode adsorption system, which can operate either in a six-bed regenerative mode at higher heat source temperatures (60–95 °C) or in a three-stage mode at lower heat source temperatures (40–60 °C). The novelty of this design lies in its ability to function under small regeneration temperature lifts (10 K) and medium lifts (50 K), thereby enabling effective utilization of low-grade solar or waste heat while simultaneously reducing chilled-water outlet temperature fluctuations and improving waste-heat recovery efficiency. In performance evaluation, the chiller was powered by low-grade heat sources in the 50–70 °C range with a cooling source maintained at 30 °C for air-conditioning applications. Comparative analysis between the six-bed adsorption chiller with and without the re-heat scheme revealed that when the heat source temperature exceeded 52 °C, the three-stage chiller with re-heat achieved higher cooling capacity and COP than the conventional three-stage design.

High-temperature heat sources (200 °C and above) can, in fact, be utilized by single-stage adsorption systems e.g., zeolite-water typically requires regeneration temperatures near 200 °C so a single cycle is not inherently impractical. The motivation for using a cascaded configuration is to better match the large temperature lift and improve overall energy utilization: two or more distinct cycles operate in series at different temperature levels and often with different working pairs. The high-temperature topping cycle is driven directly by the hot source, and its released adsorption heat (and bed sensible heat) is recovered to drive the lower-temperature bottoming cycle, thereby enhancing COP and extending the usable cooling range. Figs. 16 and 17
show a schematic and the corresponding Clapeyron diagram for a two-cycle cascaded system.

Meunier [49] examined four different configurations of cascading solid adsorber cycles operating at a high regeneration temperature of around 275 °C. In three of these configurations, a high-temperature double-effect zeolite-water cycle was used to drive a low-temperature activated carbon-methanol cycle. One such arrangement was a double-effect zeolite-water cycle that provided the necessary heat to the downstream cycle. Using this cascaded approach, cooling at about -10 °C could be achieved with a COP as high as 0.85. Douss and Meunier [50] reported experimental results on a cascaded adsorptive heat pump. The system comprised two stages: a high-temperature two-adsorber active carbon-methanol cycle and a low-temperature two-adsorber zeolite-water cycle. The zeolite adsorbers were heated by a boiler, while the adsorption heat released from the zeolite stage was recovered to drive the desorption in the active carbon-methanol stage. Under these operating conditions, the cascaded cycle achieved a COP of 1.06, which was considerably higher than the reference systems namely, a two- adsorber cycle (COP =0.75) and a simple intermittent cycle (COP = 0.50). Thus, compared to the baseline single-stage configurations, the cascaded arrangement significantly improved energy utilization. For air-conditioning applications, the cycle was shown to perform effectively as long as the evaporation temperature lift was below about 45 °C.

A low-grade heat source driven sophisticated four-bed mass recovery adsorption cooling cycle was researched by Alam et al. [39]. There were two fundamental adsorption refrigeration cycles in the cascade cycle that was suggested. All primary system components were kept at separate pressure levels because of the cascading usage of cooling and heat sources. The performance of the suggested cycle and the standard two-bed adsorption cooling cycle were compared by the authors. The suggested cycle's cooling performance was shown to be worse to a two-stage cycle for heat sources that were less than 70 °C, but its coefficient of performance (COP) was higher than a two-stage cycle for heat sources that were more than 60 °C.

In a separate study, Akahira et al. [51] investigated the functioning of a four-bed, silica gel- water mass recovery adsorption refrigeration cycle with energy cascading. Hot and cooling water were employed in a cascading flow from one desorber or adsorber to another, where the refrigerant's travel from desorber to adsorber was expedited. The investigated cascading cycle consisted of two single-stage cycles. Research was done on how operating temperature and cascading chilled water affected the system's performance. The findings showed that the suggested cycle with cascading chilled water had a higher SCP and COP than the traditional single-stage cycle and that it was highly advantageous at low heat source temperatures. Water was the refrigerant in all three of the adsorbents in Liu and Leong's [40] unique cascading adsorption cycle, which was designed for refrigeration purposes and included two zeolite and one silica gel adsorbent bed. The two zeolite adsorbent beds were used for both mass and heat recovery. Furthermore, heat was transferred between the silica gel and zeolite adsorbent beds. The best COP rose from about 1.0 at 443 K to 1.35 at 473 K, reaching nearly 1.4 at 503 K as the driving temperature increased. Nevertheless, the COP barely changed when the driving temperature was raised over 503 K.

The heat transfer fluid in a regenerative or thermal wave system flows cyclically in the following order: the heat source, the heat sink, the adsorber that has to be heated, and the adsorber that has to be cooled. A thermal wave is formed inside the adsorbers and gradually advances over time when the flow rate of the heat transfer fluid is small. The fluid experiences a significant temperature change as it passes through the adsorbers. Approximately 65 percent of the energy can be collected during this process from the cooling adsorber and transferred to the heating reactor [52]. After every half-cycle, the direction of fluid flow must be changed since the responsibilities of the adsorbers are switched. A typical thermal wave cycle system is shown in Fig. 18 .

Shelton et al. [37] performed a thermodynamic analysis for a thermally powered single-stage heat pump with two beds that uses a pair of zeolite adsorption and ammonia. The device heats and cools two solid adsorber beds using a straightforward fluid circulation heat transfer loop. The heat transfer fluid loop facilitates the passage of heat from the cooling adsorber bed to the heating desorber bed.

A visualization of a square wave is used to represent the thermal wave's actual shape. To accommodate genuine finite wave shapes, this square wave is intended to terminate before reaching the bed ends. A thermodynamic cycle that incorporates the square wave model offers comprehensive data on the beds' operation, the COP, and the heat pump cycle's heating and cooling outputs. The impact of important cycle design and operating parameters on cycle performance was investigated. The cycle's thermodynamic analysis revealed 1.87 heating coefficients of performance. Miles and Shelton [38] investigated two bed heating and cooling adsorption systems that made use of the activated carbon/ammonia combination in a different investigation. Numerous cycle variants and bed types have been constructed and tested. It was determined that the system's efficiency may be greatly increased by utilizing a thermal wave regeneration approach. Based on the experimental results, a heating seasonal COP of 1.21 and a cooling seasonal COP of 0.76 were calculated.

Ben Amar [52] numerically analyzed an adsorptive heat pump system with temperature wave heat generation using a two-dimensional model that took into account both mass and heat transport mechanisms inside the adsorber. Two adsorption systems using activated carbon AX21-ammonia and zeolite NaX-water pairs were simulated numerically. The study examined the impact of several operating factors on the system's COP and SCP, including cycle duration, permeability, and heating temperature. The findings of the simulation showed that, under optimal conditions, the temperature wave regenerative heat pump achieved a COP of 1.0–1.07 (compared to 0.6–0.7 for the uniform temperature system) and a cold production power of up to 175–200 W/kg, thereby clearly outperforming the simple heat pump. Sun et al. [53] conducted a numerical analysis of an adsorptive heat pump system incorporating thermal wave heat regeneration. Their model was two-dimensional, accounting for axial heat transfer in the circulating fluid and radial heat conduction within the adsorbent bed, while axial conduction in the bed was neglected. Using moment analysis, the authors determined time constants for heat conduction and heat exchange in the adsorbent bed, enabling a comparison of the relative importance of the two processes. The study further analyzed how cycle time and the apparent thermal conductivity of the adsorbent bed influenced system performance, showing that higher bed conductivity sharpens temperature fronts, reduces heat transfer resistance, and thereby enhances the COP and specific cooling power.

A local equilibrium model for a thermal-wave adsorption heat pump cycle powered by low temperature waste heat sources of 373–393 K was created by Sward et al. [54] in a distinct study. The model performed well asymptotically, or at the maximum feasible level. Cycle coefficients of performance (COPs) were found to be greater than 1.2 for the basic example, which had heat source temperatures of 393 K, condenser temperatures of 303 K, and evaporator temperatures of 278 K in a cycle using a water/NaX zeolite adsorbate/adsorbent pair.

Fig. 19 shows the performance indicators of the previously reported cycles. It can be observed that the simple intermittent cycle exhibits the lowest COP (0.5) and SCP (40–45 W/kg), while the introduction of heat and mass recovery significantly enhances both indicators. The cascaded cycle further improves COP beyond 1.3, although its SCP remains moderate, whereas the thermal wave cycle demonstrates a balanced performance with both high COP (1.2) and the highest SCP (90 W/kg). This comparison clearly highlights the performance improvement achieved by advanced cycle configurations over the basic intermittent system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement of heat/mass transfer in adsorption bed
ce:section.ce:para: One of the main reasons these systems have not yet been commercialized is because of the poor mass/heat transmission in the adsorption beds, which results in low performance and bigger chiller unit sizes. Therefore, to enhance the performance, external as well as internal heat transfer rate of the bed should be increased. Increasing the heat exchanger area, employing coated adsorption beds, and applying heat pipe technology are the primary strategies to improve external heat transmission in the bed. Consolidated adsorbent beds are the best choice for adsorption cooling systems in order to enhance internal heat transmission. Fig. 20 shows the heat and mass transfer enhancement techniques in adsorption beds. The strategies for improving performance mainly include enhancing heat transfer area, employing coated adsorbents and heat pipe technology, and using consolidated adsorbent beds. These approaches collectively aim to reduce thermal resistance and improve overall system efficiency.

By reducing the thickness of the adsorbent bed and incorporating extended surface areas such as fins, a higher heat transfer coefficient can be attained. Many techniques have been employed by researchers to expand the heat exchanger’s surface area in adsorption beds. For instance, Al Mers et al. optimized a cylindrical finned reactor for an activated carbon-ammonia adsorption system and reported that adding fins increased the solar COP by more than 45 % compared to a non-finned reactor [55]. Similarly, Kubota et al. developed a fin-type silica gel tube (FST) module and experimentally verified that optimized fin geometry could double the cooling output per unit adsorber volume compared to un-optimized modules [56]. Ji et al. further confirmed that aluminum-alloy finned-tube beds improved uniformity of temperature distribution and achieved a maximum COP of 0.122 with daily ice-making up to 6.5 kg in solar-powered adsorption systems [57].

However, a major disadvantage of enlarging the heat exchanger surface is the associated increase in the thermal mass of the adsorber, which raises heat capacity and lowers the effective coefficient of performance (COP). Therefore, combining surface enlargement with advanced cycles or optimized configurations is essential to achieve meaningful performance enhancement.

Using the plate-fin heat exchanger as an adsorption bed is one of the best ways to increase the heat exchanger area. As seen in Fig. 21 , the plate-finned bed can have several structural configurations, including the fin plate type [58], the finned tube type [55–57], and the flat-pipe type [59,60]. A number of plate-fin heat exchange units make up the plate-finned adsorption bed. Thin fins are present on the adsorption bed's fluid and adsorbent sides to enhance the surface available for heat exchange. The fins on the adsorbent side split the adsorbent into several tiny units. The plate walls and fin that encircle the adsorbent efficiently increase the adsorbent side's heat transfer capability. Heat transmission is accelerated by the turbulence created by the pores on the fluid-side fins, which also disrupt the thermal and flow boundary layers.

Additionally, the contact thermal resistance is decreased by the pressure differential between the fluid and adsorbent sides. The impact of the finned tube on the solar energy-driven adsorber performance was investigated by Louajari et al. [61]. The machine with fins performed better than the one without, according to the results. The adsorber with fins reached a maximum temperature of 97 °C, whereas the one without fins only reached 77 °C. In accordance, the COP was raised from 0.075 to 0.111.

The spiral plate adsorbent bed is compact and having high heat transfer rate. Its structure is shown in Fig. 22 . High heat flux is produced as a result of the sandwich construction, which creates a homogenous temperature difference between the thermal fluid and the adsorbent material. When it comes to basic beds, this one requires a lower driving temperature. Nonetheless, the operating temperature and pressure must not be excessively high for these beds. It's not too easy to seal and fix the bed body. Filling the adsorbent into the bed also requires more work. An adsorptive refrigerator employing a spiral-style adsorbent bed was examined by Wang et al. [60]. The designed refrigerator, according to the authors, can generate more than 1 kg of ice for every kg of adsorbent in a single day. One day following additional advancements, it was anticipated that the ice-making power density would be 2–4 kg of ice per kg of adsorbent.

Porous adsorbent beds are made to improve heat transfer and lower mass transfer resistance by having many linked pores and tunnels that let adsorbate vapour travel more freely through the structure [62]. This design makes the effective heat transfer area and vapor permeability bigger, which makes the whole system work better.

There have been a number of ways to make permeable beds:
i. Solid sintering: Under controlled heat and pressure, adsorbent powders such activated carbon, zeolite, and silica gel are sintered together to make a rigid porous matrix. This method makes sure that the structure is stable and has good heat conductivity.
ii. Melt solidification: Particles that can adsorb are put into a molten media, like metal or polymer, which then hardens to make a composite porous structure that is stronger.
iii. Metal deposition: Thin coatings of metal are put on the surface of adsorbent grains. This makes the grains more thermally conductive while keeping their porosity.
iv. Making corrosion pores: Controlled chemical or electrochemical corrosion is used to make holes and tunnels in metal or composite adsorbers, which increases both the surface area and the permeability.

Porous beds have a number of benefits, such as low specific heat capacity, low thermal contact resistance with the metallic sealing wall, and rapid rates of heat and mass transfer. But making them is typically hard and expensive, which makes it hard for them to be used by many people.

The plate-fin bed's increased area is less than the pin-fin bed's. The temperature field's homogeneity is improved and the total transfer resistance is decreased due to the larger area. Fig. 23 displays the structural diagram of a normal pin-fin bed. The rich secondary extended surface of the acupuncture is used by the pin-fin bed's heat transfer enhancement mechanism to improve heat transmission. The pin-fin's surface area is increased by 22.36 % when compared to the plate-fin at the same weight [63]. As a result, there is less additional cooling or heating load on the pin-fin bed, which makes it more compact. Furthermore, because the adsorbent is immediately injected into the acupuncture space, it simultaneously improves heat transfer and increases the density of the adsorbent for a given volume.

Although zeolites and silica gel are inexpensive adsorbents, they require improved heat transfer rates and water absorption for the chiller to operate in real time. In order to accelerate the rate of desorption, Qadir et al. [64] added multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) to MIL- 100(Fe). They also used the proposed MWCNT/MIL-100(Fe) composite adsorbent to model the performance of a two-bed solar adsorption chiller through the numerical solution of energy balance and mass balance equations in conjunction with the Sips model and the linear driving force (LDF) correlation. The study revealed that the adsorption chiller's maximum possible cooling power of 455 W/kg is achieved by using the MWCNT/MIL-100(Fe) composite adsorbent, which contains 6.1 % MWCNTs. The suggested MWCNT/MIL-100(Fe) composite adsorbent was found to be a viable substitute for the traditional zeolite- and silica gel-based adsorbents in the upcoming generation of high-performance solar-powered adsorption chillers. Kim et al. [65] developed an aluminophosphate (AlPO4)-based adsorbent (Fig. 24 ) for water adsorption chiller. Because it lacks silicon species in its structure, this adsorbent demonstrated a significantly higher working capacity during both the adsorption and desorption processes than silico-aluminophosphate (SAPO4) adsorbent. In hot and muggy conditions, while a water adsorption chiller is operating, the aluminophosphate is incredibly stable. The findings of the experiment and simulation demonstrated that the aluminophosphate adsorbent's SCP is 28 % more than the silico-aluminophosphate adsorbent's and silica gel's simulated SCPs.

Zeolites and silica gel are cheap and often used adsorbents, but their low thermal conductivity makes systems less efficient. This has led to the creation of composite beds that improve heat and mass transmission. For instance, integrating carbon nanotubes into metal-organic frameworks have been found to considerably enhance thermal conductivity and obtain specific cooling values as high as 455 W/kg. Consolidated silica gel-graphite composites have shown almost three times higher thermal conductivity than loose beds. This has led to big gains in cooling capacity and cycle stability. More recently, adsorbents made of alumina phosphate have been able to cool things down around 28 % better than regular silica gel or silicon alumina phosphate materials, while still being very stable in hot and humid circumstances. These examples show that composite adsorbents, whether they are made of carbon nanostructures, conductive additives, or new phosphate frameworks, could lead to the next generation of high- performance adsorption refrigeration systems.

Fig. 25 summarizes three major categories of composite beds: (i) carbon-based composites such as CNTs integrated into MOFs, which deliver very high specific cooling powers; (ii) graphite-consolidated composites, where the addition of graphite significantly enhances thermal conductivity; and (iii) phosphate-based composites such as aluminophosphates, which exhibit improved stability and higher SCP compared to conventional silica gel and zeolite adsorbents.

In buildings, the space is of major concern so A small dual adsorber adsorption cooling system was constructed by Chan et al. [62] using water as the adsorbate and zeolite 13X/CaCl2 composite adsorbent. To improve the heat and mass transfer processes, they built adsorbers by directly coating the composite adsorbent on finned heat exchangers with parallel flow. Even though the cooling water temperature was raised from 22 °C to 28 °C, the new adsorber design significantly boosted SCP from 106 W/kg to 377 W/kg (a 256 % improvement) at the same desorption temperature and chilled water inlet temperature. Additionally, the COP has increased from 0.16 to 0.27. The ACS has the potential to be a viable substitute and can be implemented in buildings to lower the energy used for heating and cooling with further system improvement. Adsorption cooling systems based on activated carbon have not been widely used because of the material's low packing density and inadequate heat transfer characteristics. When multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) were added to an AC adsorbent, Jahan et al. [66] observed 2.47 and 3.5-times higher packing densities and thermal conductivities than the parent AC. The incorporation of MWCNT resulted in an improved adsorption of CO2, and hence cooling performances. The research contributes to the creation of a small, effective, and ecologically safe adsorption cooling system.

An adsorption cooling system using a new adsorption packed bed made up of repeated packed bed modules with silica gel RD-2060/water and AQSOA-Z02/water was studied by Mohammed et al. [67] as shown in Fig. 26 . In terms of a higher SCP, silica gel has been suggested as an adsorbent over AQSOA-Z02; it has been discovered that the SCP produced is 2.3 times higher than that of conventional adsorption chillers. They stated that the modular design could result in reduced manufacturing costs and allow air conditioning systems to be scaled for varying heat loads.

In order to enhance the cooling performance of adsorption cooling system, Ilis et al. [68] developed an innovative star fin type adsorbent bed as shown in Fig. 27 with metal additives and studied the optimum geometrical parameters of the bed with Fuji RD-type silica gel/water working pair. According to the study, the best adsorbent bed had the highest SCP of 14.8 W/kg. Adding 40 % of aluminum particles could boost cooling efficiency by 3.3 times, from 14.8 to 41.2 W/kg.

Silica gel/water-based adsorption cooling system has low thermal performances due to low thermal conductivity of silica gel. Mohammed et al. [69] improved the heat and mass transport characteristics of the adsorption bed by incorporating silica-gel particles into a high-porosity aluminum foam, as seen in Fig. 26. They discovered that the suggested adsorption cooling (AD) chiller has many times higher specific cooling power (SCP), cooling capacity per unit volume (CPv), and COP. In actuality, aluminum foam considerably lowers the bed's conductive heat resistance. It was proposed that aluminum foam may be used to construct AD systems that are both more compact and efficient. (Fig. 28 )

Fig. 29 illustrates different untreated adsorbent bed designs. (a) A cylindrical bed with heat medium tubes and rib pieces, where the adsorbent is contained between metallic nets. (b) A photographic view of an activated carbon fiber (A-20) based adsorbent layer along with its module layout. (c) A schematic of a finned stainless-steel shell packed with monolithic carbon and aluminum fins, providing enhanced thermal conductivity and optimized gas flow paths.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heat pipe use in adsorption systems
ce:section.ce:para: Among the restrictions on the commercial use of adsorption cooling systems are the high costs and poor heat transmission capabilities of the adsorbers. The utilization of heat pipes has the potential to mitigate the aforementioned issues. This is because, in addition to offering a higher heat flux density, these devices eliminate the need for moving elements to drive the heat transfer medium, resulting in a more dependable and affordable system overall [71]. The schematic of the heat pipe adsorber is shown in Fig. 30 .

Meunier [72] reported that the use of heat pipes in adsorption systems at LIMSI yielded very high heat transfer coefficients approaching 10 kW/m². Similarly, Critoph [73] employed a heat pipe to heat and cool adsorption beds, concluding that to avoid problems related to sub- atmospheric or excessively high working pressures, different heating and cooling fluids should be selected according to their physical properties, with thicker wall materials for containment. Several subsequent studies have confirmed the potential of heat pipe integration. For example, Wang et al. designed a calcium chloride-activated carbon composite-ammonia system in which the adsorber heat pipes significantly improved cycle stability and reduced sensible heat losses [70]. Lu et al. developed a dual-bed multipurpose heat pipe adsorption chiller, tested with acetone and water as heat pipe working fluids, and demonstrated that water offered better performance when driven by diesel engine exhaust heat [74]. In another design, Restuccia et al. tested a compact coated tube-fin adsorber combined with heat pipe technology, and observed a reduction of cycle time to 15–20 min compared to more than one hour in conventional pellet beds [75]. Furthermore, Chang et al. analyzed silica gel-coated heat exchanger beds and showed that coupling thin sorbent layers with embedded heat pipes minimized thermal resistance and enhanced regeneration performance [76]. Overall, these studies indicate that the combination of coated or consolidated adsorbent beds with embedded heat pipe technology can achieve significantly higher heat transfer rates, reduced cycle times, and improved SCP and COP, making heat pipe adsorbers a promising direction for advancing adsorption refrigeration technology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Eliminating the adsorbent's and wall's resistance to contact
ce:section.ce:para: The thermal contact resistance between the adsorbent and the metal wall of the heat exchanger can make up over a fourth of the total heat transfer resistance, which makes the cycle work much less well [70]. To get over this problem, a number of methods have been created to reduce or get rid of the interfacial barrier. One option that has been investigated a lot is to apply adsorbents directly to the surfaces of heat exchangers. Experimental studies demonstrated that coated beds can attain thermal conductivity up to four times greater than that of packed beds utilizing the same adsorbent material [77,78]. Restuccia et al. put hydrophobic zeolite coatings on finned tube heat exchangers, for example, and found that the cycle time dropped from 60- 120 min to just 15–20 min, with specific cooling powers of 30–60 W/kg about six times higher than pelletized beds [75]. Chang et al. [76] showed that the thickness of the silica gel covering and the size of the particles had an effect on the results. Their findings demonstrated that thinner coatings coupled with larger particles diminished contact resistance, improved regeneration rates, and sustained consistent system performance. Waszkiewicz et al. [79] also looked into coated annular fins. They found that zeolite layers that bonded to aluminum substrates had heat transfer coefficients of about 3000 W/m²K, while packed beds only had heat transfer coefficients of about 25 W/m²K. These results underscore the considerable benefit of elongated, coated surfaces in diminishing interfacial resistance.

Researchers have also looked into consolidated or monolithic adsorbent structures in addition to coatings. Graphite-silica gel composites and metal foam-zeolite beds have demonstrated several-fold enhancements in thermal conductivity compared to granular beds, while preserving enough porosity for vapor movement [78]. Ji et al. verified that consolidated finned- tube adsorbers resulted in more uniform temperature distributions and increased cycle stability, hence improving SCP in solar-powered settings [57]. Direct crystallization is another promising method. In this method, zeolite layers are formed on stainless steel substrates using hydrothermal methods. Schnabel et al. [78] showed that these zeolite coatings that crystallized in place had better adhesion, got rid of binder layers, and made adsorption kinetics better than laminated structures.

In general, these studies show that getting rid of or reducing wall-adsorbent contact resistance through coating methods, consolidated composites, coated fins, or direct crystallization greatly improves heat transfer, shortens cycle times, and increases both specific cooling power and coefficient of performance in adsorption refrigeration systems. Fig. 31 shows the approaches to reduce thermal contact resistance between the adsorbent and wall (a) Heat transfer model illustrating resistances in the tube wall and adsorbent layer, where overall performance is governed by conduction and interfacial contact. (b) Photographs of consolidated adsorbent composites with improved thermal pathways. (c) Cylindrical adsorbent compacts demonstrating enhanced structural stability and thermal conductivity. (d) Prototype adsorber module using consolidated beds to minimize wall-adsorbent interfacial resistance and improve heat transfer efficiency [78].

To fix the fact that adsorbent beds don't conduct heat well, many additives have been added to adsorbent materials. The goal of these additions is to make heat transmission better without significantly lowering the ability to adsorb. Researchers have shown that adding metallic particles like copper, aluminum, stainless steel, or brass to adsorbent matrices greatly improves heat conductivity. For example, beds with copper and aluminum alloys had cooling capacities that were 11–12 % better and COPs that were 2–3 % better, mostly because the metallic phases had better conductivity [80–82]. Fang et al. constructed an economical adsorption chiller utilizing aluminum corrugated heat exchangers coated with a LiCl-WSS composite, achieving a COP of 0.45 and a SCP of 0.41 kW/kg under standard regeneration settings [83]. Other experiments found that aluminum and stainless-steel inserts sped up desorption rates. Spiral- shaped parts worked even better since they had more surface area exposed, improving performance by more than 20 % compared to regular beds as shown in Fig. 32 .

Graphite-based additives have also gotten a lot of attention, along with metals. Expanded graphite, natural graphite flakes, and graphite fibers have significant thermal conductivity on their own and create conductive channels in the adsorbent structure. Adsorbent composites made with graphite can have thermal conductivities that are many times higher than those of pure adsorbents. This shortens cycle times and improves SCP. Also, consolidated adsorbent- graphite blocks have good thermal performance and are stable mechanically, which makes them good for use in real-world adsorption chillers.

Researchers have also looked into carbon-based additions including carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and activated carbon fibers. Composites using CNTs not only have better thermal conductivity, but they can also have more adsorption sites, which means they can cool things down more effectively. In a single investigation, a CNT-MOF composite reached SCP values above 450 W/kg, which is far better than typical zeolite-silica gel beds. In general, adding thermally conductive additives to adsorbent beds, whether they are made of metal or carbon, is a potential way to get around problems with heat transfer. Metal chips, wires, or spiral pieces make things stronger and improve conductivity by a small amount. Graphite and carbon nanomaterials, on the other hand, have better conductivity and adsorption synergy, which lets adsorption refrigeration systems work faster and have a higher COP. Table 3 provides a summary of the performances of AC systems with different adsorbent bed types.
1. Normalization of gains: %ΔCOP=(COPnew-COPbase)/COPbase × 100 %; %ΔSCP=(SCPnew-SCPbase)/SCPbase × 100 %
2. Baseline definition: “Baseline bed” refers to the unenhanced configuration explicitly stated in the source; when the baseline is not reported, gains are marked NR.
3. Operating conditions: Where hot/cooling/chilled-water temperatures (Th/Tc/Te) and cycle time are absent, values are marked NR; direct cross-study comparison should prioritize entries with reported boundary conditions.
4. Manufacturability classes used here: packed powder/granules; coated heat exchanger; consolidated/monolith; metal foam/graphite matrix; heat pipe integration; additive- manufactured structures.
5. Durability risks: include binder aging, coating delamination, salt swelling/migration (for salt-hybrids), corrosion/compatibility (notably NH₃), and long-term thermal cycling.
