Table 2 shows that bed-design improvements are mostly beneficial in terms of minimizing the thermal resistance (fins, metal casings, conductive additives) or minimizing the diffusion path, although this is achieved at an evident cost in manufacturability and durability. The low-cost additive techniques (metal inserts/additives) generally can provide small COP benefits with a low fabrication price, and structured heat exchangers (plate-finned/tube-finned) can offer larger benefits but raise the cost of metal, assembly and pressure-drop limits. In the case of ammonia- based pairs, corrosion/compatibility and seal reliability take the role of first-order durability risks and tend to dominate the feasibility decision of practical adoption regardless of good heat- transfer behavior.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AI model for predicting performance of adsorption system
ce:section.ce:para: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is defined as calculations that allow a machine to emulate features of human intelligence, including learning, reasoning, and decision-making, which have drawn the interest of researchers in the sphere of thermal sciences due to its greater efficiency and effectiveness in resolving highly nonlinear, multi-variable tasks compared with traditional methods. High cost, small sizes and low performance is the biggest problem that has held back the commercialization of adsorption cooling system. It remains a difficult task to improve performance because designs of beds, the development of materials and the flow of operations remain complex and can only be analysed, computed and experimented in complicated, costly and time-consuming ways [85–88].

In order to skip these hurdles, AI-assisted technologies have proven to be potent in the design, predict and optimization of adsorption systems. In contrast to classical computational fluid dynamics or purely empirical approaches, AI can learn both from experimental and simulated evidence, enabling precise modelling at lower time and cost. Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), or electrically more broadly the deep learning algorithms, have already been utilized successfully to forecast performance of adsorption systems, modelling cycle dynamics, and finding optimal cycle design points. Figs. 33 and 34
represent the outline of an artificial neuron and schematic of a multi-layer neural network respectively. It should also be noted that ANN is not the only line of contemporary AI. Further developed methods, including reinforcement learning, evolutionary algorithms and AI-simulation systems, are in use in the energy system now and have a high potential to adsorption refrigeration as well. The connection of ANN applications to the broader context of AI illustrates one of the ways to go beyond narrow predictive models towards large-scale intelligent systems capable of operating adsorption cycles in real-time, finding optimal operational strategies with a changing set of boundaries and even proposing novel material-cycle couplings [89–94].

The broader-system-scale implication of AI would be a system-level integration of adsorption cooling to renewable energy sources, smart grids and building energy management systems, where predictive control and adaptive scheduling become possible. It can be projected that AI in adsorption technology has already a roadmap: (i) machine learning surrogates (with prediction of adsorption equilibria and kinetics); (ii) optimization of bed designs and the cycle strategies through hybrid AI-simulation models; (iii) self-learned controllers (operational self- management of large systems); (iv) integration with digital twins (large-scale deployment of smart energy networks). The promise inherent in AI as a modelling, rather than a radical, enabler of scalable, efficient, intelligent adsorption cooling, is highlighted throughout this progressive roadmap [95–99].

Adsorption refrigeration systems are operating in a completely new way thanks to Digital Twin (DT) technology. A digital twin is an AI-powered, real-time virtual duplicate of a physical adsorption chiller that continuously receives data from embedded sensors in the real system. Digital twins, which combine physics-based simulations, machine-learning models, and Internet of Things connectivity, accurately replicate system behavior even in the face of fluctuating loads, environmental factors, and input heat fluctuations. This feature guarantees fast fault detection, enabling operators to spot problems like refrigerant leaks, bed deterioration, or valve malfunctions before they get worse. Additionally, predictive maintenance is made possible by digital twins, which allow operators to plan servicing in advance to avoid malfunctions, reduce downtime, and prolong component life.

Furthermore, by examining thousands of operational scenarios to identify ideal cycle switching times, heat recovery tactics, and regeneration conditions, DT frameworks excel in real-time energy optimization. Digital twins are therefore crucial for district cooling networks, smart building integration, and renewable energy-based thermal management since they greatly improve the dependability, efficiency, and control capabilities of adsorption refrigeration systems.

An artificial neural network (ANN) model was created by Baiju and Muraleedharan [100] to analyze the performance of a single-stage solar adsorption refrigeration system using activated carbon and R134a as the working pair. With temperature, pressure, and solar insolation in the input layer, the back propagation method serves as the foundation for this model. To determine the optimal method, this algorithm's three variations-the Scaled conjugate gradient, the Pola- Ribiere conjugate gradient, and the Levenberg-Marquardt (LM) and logistic sigmoid transfer function-were applied. The best algorithm for simulating a solar adsorption cooling system was determined to be the LM algorithm with nine neurons after training. The greatest percentage of error was less than 5 %, and the ANN's predictions for performance parameters showed good agreement with experimental results, with R2 values approaching 1. The study came to the conclusion that the model could be accurately modeled [101–103]. The performance parameters of adsorption cooling systems, including coefficient of performance, specific cooling power, and solar cooling coefficient of performance, were predicted using the artificial neural network (ANN) approach by Baiju and Muraleedharan [104].

Three criteria-correlation coefficient, coefficient of variance, and root mean square error-were used to assess how well ANN predictions performed. With correlation coefficients close to 1 and percentage errors less than 5 %, the network model predictions and experimental values agreed well. This study saves extensive testing and the development of complex mathematical models by assisting researchers in predicting the performance of the SAR system at various evaporator loads with a minimum number of experiments.

Using waste heat sources that are almost at room temperature, Krzywanski et al. [105] built and studied a Tri-bed twin-evaporator adsorption cooler in 2017. They found that this system is the most effective technique to produce chilled water from such heat sources. The enhancement of the system's cooling capacity (CC) remains a difficult issue, and the intricacy of the tri-bed twin-evaporator adsorption chiller functioning is still not fully understood. For the optimization study of the developed three-bed adsorption chiller, they presented a non-iterative AI approach. This allowed them to examine how operating parameters affected the chiller's cooling capacity. They discovered that the model results agreed well with the experimental data, with the maximum relative error between the calculated and measured data being less than ±10 %.

For a two-stage adsorption chiller that reheats utilizing a low temperature heat source from cogeneration in the 50–90 °C range, an ANFIS model was introduced [106]. This non-iterative model predicts cooling capacity (CC), one of the primary energy-efficient components in cooling production, over a wide variety of operating and design parameters. It also defines the behavior of the adsorption cooler. The re-heat two-stage adsorption cooler's cooling performance can be estimated with this user-friendly and effective optimization tool. In order to optimize a two-bed adsorption cooler in a combined heating and cooling system powered by low-temperature heat from cogeneration, J. Krzywanski and colleagues proposed the AI technique [107]. The behavior of the cooling system under various operation scenarios and situations was examined using the created AI models. Based on the aforementioned research on AI methodologies, it can be inferred that the neurocomputing approach is a user-friendly and effective optimization tool for the adsorption chiller functioning inside the intricate HC system, potentially mitigating the drawbacks of computational and experimental methods [108–111]. Table 4 presents AI used in adsorption system analysis.

Section 4 focuses on AI for system-level performance prediction, optimization, and control once a working pair is selected. Section 5 complements this by detailing AI-enabled working- pair discovery and down-selection, explicitly linking predicted material properties (Δw, Tdes,min, kinetics, and stability) to the building operating windows and validation steps

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced discovery of adsorbent materials through AI
ce:section.ce:para: Recent advancements in artificial intelligence have significantly accelerated the development and optimization of adsorbent materials for adsorption refrigeration systems. Traditional material screening methods can be labor-intensive, slow, and costly, particularly for porous structures such as metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), zeolites, silica gels, and activated carbons. Fig. 35 presents the AI-enabled working-pair discovery pipeline for net-zero building adsorption cooling.

This pipeline links AI-based materials screening to system-relevant performance metrics (Δw, Tdes, min, SCP potential, and Carnot fraction), ensuring candidate working pairs are evaluated under building-relevant operating windows and validated through cycling and prototype bed tests.

AI-driven techniques especially neural networks, graph-based machine learning (Graph-NN), and data-driven surrogate models are high-throughput predictions of various properties are now feasible. These properties include adsorption behavior, pore structure characteristics, thermal stability, and refrigerant compatibility. Graph neural networks, in particular, can effectively represent the complex 3D topologies of MOFs and accurately predict essential properties such as diffusivity, isosteric heat, and adsorption capacity by capturing both atomic connectivity and pore geometry. This innovative approach streamlines the process of discovering new adsorbent materials, enhancing efficiency and reducing costs.

The training of deep-learning surrogates that can predict MOF-refrigerant compatibility is made possible by large adsorption databases and simulation datasets. This makes it possible to quickly identify the best material-working pair combinations for water, methanol, ethanol, or low-GWP refrigerants [112,113]. By significantly reducing the need for molecular simulations and laboratory measurements, these AI-enabled frameworks speed up the design cycle and direct researchers toward materials with high capacity, quick kinetics, and low regeneration temperature essential characteristics for next-generation, high-efficiency adsorption cooling systems [114–116].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Case example 1: ML-assisted MOF down-selection for low-grade heat
ce:section.ce:para: In one representative workflow, a machine-learning surrogate was trained on multi- temperature water adsorption isotherms of porous adsorbents (including MOFs) to predict usable working capacity (Δw) within a building window (Tevap = 10–15 °C; Tcond/Tads = 30- 35 °C) and to estimate the minimum regeneration temperature (Tdes,min) required to reach a target cycling capacity. Candidates were ranked via multi-objective screening to favor high Δw at Tdes ≤ 80–90 °C, then experimentally validated using targeted isotherm measurements and short-cycle adsorption/desorption tests. The key outcome was a shortlist of MOF-based candidates with favorable low-temperature water sorption “step” behavior, implying improved cooling potential under solar thermal/waste heat. However, the remaining barriers were hydrothermal durability under repeated cycling, shaping/coating impacts (loss of porosity or reduced kinetics), and system-level confirmation of COP/SCP under standardized boundary conditions [94,98]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Case example 2: active learning / uncertainty-aware screening with kinetics included
ce:section.ce:para: A second class of studies extended screening beyond equilibrium uptake by incorporating kinetics descriptors (e.g., LDF coefficients or uptake time constants) and using uncertainty- aware models (e.g., GPR) to recommend the next experiments via active learning. This approach explicitly targets SCP potential, since fast uptake/release is required to exploit Δw at practical cycle times. The workflow predicted not only candidate materials but also operating conditions (pressure/temperature ranges) where kinetics remain favorable; subsequent lab measurements reduced prediction uncertainty and refined the Pareto front. Reported gains are typically expressed as an expanded feasible region (higher SCP at a fixed Tdes), but long-term issues, binder aging, coating delamination, and performance drift under humid cycling, remain the main unknowns before building deployment.

This review shows that adsorption refrigeration can become a practical pathway for sustainable and net-zero buildings when working-pair selection, cycle architecture, and bed-scale intensification are treated as a single design problem and evaluated under building-relevant operating windows. While notable gains in COP and SCP have been reported through heat/mass recovery cycles, multi-bed configurations, and enhanced heat-transfer beds, translation to real building systems remains constrained by durability, manufacturability, and power-density limits. To accelerate deployment, the following research targets are prioritized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Prioritized research targets toward building deployment (ranked)

ce:list-item.ce:para: 1) Durability and cycling stability (highest priority). Performance drift remains underreported and is a major barrier to bankable building deployment, especially for MOFs under humid cycling, salt-hybrid swelling/migration, and composite binder fatigue.

ce:section.ce:para:
Quantified targets / reporting expectation:
•
Report ≥ 1000 cycles minimum for academic claims; ≥ 10,000 cycles for pre-commercial prototypes.
•
Require capacity retention ≥ 90 % (Δw or cooling capacity) with ≤ 10 % degradation in kinetics indicators (e.g., t₉₀ or effective mass transfer coefficient).
•
Include failure-mode reporting: hydrothermal collapse, salt deliquescence/swelling, corrosion/compatibility, coating delamination, and binder aging.
2) Manufacturability and scale-up of intensified beds. Many high-performing beds rely on lab-scale coatings or composites that are not yet reproducible at module scale. Scale-up bottlenecks include coating thickness control, adhesion, crack resistance, and pressure drop in structured beds.

Quantified targets / deployment expectation:
•
Demonstrate repeatable kW-scale modules (not only W/kg bench results) with defined adsorbent mass and heat-exchanger geometry.
•
Manufacturing metrics to report: coating thickness tolerance (±10–20 %), adhesion/cycling integrity, and cost-relevant steps (binder %, curing/sintering, additive manufacturing time).
3) Compactness and volumetric power density for building integration. Building adoption depends less on peak COP and more on kW per footprint/volume and system mass.

Quantified targets:
•
Report volumetric cooling power density CPv (kW/m³) and areal density (kW/m² footprint).
•
Demonstrate ≥ 1–3 kW/m³ (near-term target) and progress toward ≥ 5–10 kW/m³ (long- term) under building boundary conditions (Tevap = 10–15 °C; Tcond = 30–35 °C; Tdes ≤ 90 °C).
4) Standardized test and reporting protocol (enabler across all studies). Cross-study comparisons remain difficult due to missing temperatures, cycle time, adsorbent mass, or inconsistent SCP definitions.

Minimum reporting set (should be mandatory):
•
Tdes/Tcond/Tevap (or Th/Tc/Te), cycle time, adsorbent mass, heat-recovery/mass- recovery description, number of beds, and SCP definition (W/kg adsorbent).
•
Report Carnot fraction (COP/COPcarnot ) to normalize across temperature lifts and prevent overinterpretation of high COP at mild conditions.
5) AI integration as a closed-loop design tool (materials → system → operation). AI impact will be strongest when used as a pipeline: screening working pairs under building windows, optimizing cycle/bed design, and enabling digital-twin control for reliability and efficiency.
Near-term target: AI-assisted surrogate models to map COP-SCP-cycle time trade-offs under fixed Tevap/Tcond/Tdes windows.
Long-term target: Digital twin + predictive control integrated with active learning for materials and bed validation (closing the screening-to-prototype loop).
Table 5 presents the roadmap from research challenges to deployable adsorption cooling modules for net-zero buildings.

Overall, the fastest route to building deployment is a durability-first, manufacturability-aware development pathway: (i) screen working pairs using Δw-Tdes,min maps within building windows, (ii) select cycle architectures based on Carnot-normalized performance, and (iii) realize compactness through scalable bed designs with validated long-term cycling. AI should be positioned not as a standalone topic but as the integrating layer that connects material discovery, system co-design, and operational control into a closed-loop optimization framework.

Ramesh P Sah: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Anirban Sur: Supervision, Software, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Naresh Chaudhari: Validation, Resources, Methodology. Ashok Kumar Yadav: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Aqueel Ahmad: Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ashu Yadav: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors of the manuscript entitled “Advanced Adsorbent-Adsorbate Pairs for Sustainable and Energy-Efficient Adsorption Refrigeration in Net-Zero Buildings: AI-Driven Material Innovations, System Integration, and Future Perspectives” have no conflict of interest. All authors have agreed for the submission.
